menu_title: Dead Air -- The Two Minutes Before the Call Starts
menu_desc: Small talk with Americans: open, react, add, and bridge to business
title_sub: The Two Minutes Before the Call Really Starts
ch3_sub: The camera is on, the client is there, and the meeting has not started
ch5_sub: She is distracted, and she answers in three words
ch6_sub: Quick fire, three role-plays, and a client who answers "fine"

plan_row.tema: Dead Air -- The Two Minutes Before the Call Starts
plan_row.foco: Small talk questions (How is your week going?) + question tags (isn't it?) + bridge (anyway / by the way)
plan_row.atividade: Two-minute warm-up + role-play with a client who gives one-word answers
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min warm-up: open, react, add one thing about you, bridge

heading: The Hardest English of
All
warmup.intro: Last class you ran a kickoff, and you closed like this:

callback.text: Can we agree on that today, in writing?
callback.speak: Can we agree on that today, in writing?

warmup.box_title: Today there is no agenda, no artefact, and nothing to defend.
warmup.box_text: It is 2:58. You and Sarah, the client's project manager in Chicago, are alone on the call. Two minutes of silence. In business English, this is the hardest thing there is.

goal.d: Eight words for the small talk

heading: The Language of Small
Talk
vocab_ch.sub: 8 words to fill two minutes, and sound human

vocab.def: To make the first, easy conversation
vocab.def_pc: to make the first, easy conversation
vocab.hint: To make the first, easy conversation
vocab.ex: Somebody has to break the ice, and today it is you.

vocab.def: To hear a person's news, after some time
vocab.def_pc: to hear a person's news, after some time
vocab.hint: To hear someone's news after a while
vocab.ex: It is good to catch up before we start.

vocab.def: An easy, friendly conversation about nothing important
vocab.def_pc: an easy, friendly conversation about nothing important
vocab.ex: We had a quick chat about the weekend.
Silence on a video call is awkward.
Keep it casual: this is not the meeting yet.

vocab.def: Used to add something, or to change the topic
vocab.def_pc: used to add something, or to change the topic
vocab.hint: Used to add or change topic
vocab.ex: By the way, how was the conference?

vocab.def: Used to close the small talk and start the meeting
vocab.def_pc: used to close the small talk and start the meeting
vocab.ex: Anyway, shall we start? We have a full agenda.

vocab.def: Saturday and Sunday -- the safest topic there is
vocab.def_pc: Saturday and Sunday: the safest topic there is
vocab.ex: Did you have a good weekend?

heading: The Silence You
Remember
vocab_check.prompt: Think of a call where nobody spoke for two minutes, and you did not know what to say.
vocab_check.sub: Talk for 90 seconds: who was there, what did you say in the end, and what did you wish you had said?

heading: Why the Silence Costs You the
Client
listenings.text: Diogo, a word before your call with Chicago. I know you think small talk is a waste of time. In the United States it is not: it is how people decide if they trust you. Two minutes, no more, but you have to do it. Ask an open question -- how is your week going, how was the weekend. Then react to what she says, and add something about yourself, or it becomes an interview. Safe topics: weather, weekend, travel, sport, food. Dangerous topics: politics, salary, religion, and the age of anybody. And when it is time, close it yourself. Say anyway, and start the meeting. If you do not close it, the small talk eats your agenda.
comp: Why is small talk not a waste of time in the United States?
Because it is how people decide if they trust you.
What must Diogo do after she answers?
React to her answer, and add something about himself -- or it becomes an interview.
Politics, salary, religion, and anybody's age.

heading: Two Minutes,
Done Well
listenings.lead: This is the target. Listen, then do it your way.
listenings.text: Hi Sarah, good to see you. How is your week going so far? ... Busy, I can imagine -- it is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it? Mine started badly: my flight from Sao Paulo was three hours late, so I had a lot of time to read at the airport. Did you have a good weekend, by the way? ... Oh really? I did not know you played tennis. My daughter started last year, and now she wins against me, which is not good for my ego. Chicago must be cold for tennis in June, no? ... Right. Anyway, we have a full agenda today, so shall we start? I will share my screen, and I would like to leave ten minutes at the end for your questions.
comp: What does he say about his own week?
It started badly: his flight was three hours late.
How does he react to the tennis?
He adds something personal: his daughter plays, and she beats him.
How does he close the small talk?
He says "anyway", mentions the full agenda, and starts the meeting.

heading: The Two-Minute
Warm-Up
artefact.title: Two Minutes. Four Moves.
artefact.reason: Open: How is your week going? / Did you have a good weekend?
rows: React: "Oh really?" / "That sounds great." / "I can imagine."
Add one thing about you -- or it becomes an interview
Safe: weather, weekend, travel, sport, food. Dangerous: politics, salary, religion, age
Bridge: "Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?"
artefact.footer: Two minutes, and you close it yourself. Small talk that has no end eats the meeting.

grammar.prompt: Which one opens? Which one keeps it alive? Which one ends it?

discovery.html: "How is your week going so far?"
discovery.speak: How is your week going so far?

discovery.html: "It is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it?"
discovery.speak: It is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it?

discovery.html: "By the way, did you have a good weekend?"
discovery.speak: By the way, did you have a good weekend?

discovery.html: "Anyway, shall we start? We have a full agenda."
discovery.speak: Anyway, shall we start? We have a full agenda.

rows: How is + your week / your day + going? (word order!)
How is your week going so far?
React (Oh really? / I can imagine.) + a question tag: isn't it? / doesn't it?
Busy quarter for you too, isn't it?
grammar.note: Small talk is not about the weather. It is a test: can this person have a normal human conversation? Answer the question, then add something about yourself -- an answer with no addition kills the conversation, and makes it feel like an interrogation.
mistakes: It is raining in Chicago, no?
It is raining in Chicago, isn't it?
grammar.mistake_note: A ordem é How is your week going? (sujeito antes do verbo -ing). E o "né?" do português vira question tag: is + not → isn't it?, does + not → doesn't it?

heading: How to Fill Two
Minutes
bank.sub: Open → react → add something about you → one more question → bridge to the agenda
bank.label: The five lines of a warm-up
items: Hi Sarah, good to see you. How is your week going so far?
Busy, I can imagine -- it is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it?
Mine started badly: my flight was three hours late.
Did you have a good weekend, by the way?
Oh really? I did not know you played tennis.
Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?

scenarios: You join the call five minutes early. The client's CTO is already there, alone, with his camera on.
You meet the American team in person for the first time, in the lift, going to the meeting room.
The client mentions that her daughter is starting university in August. Keep the conversation alive for one minute.
scenarios_sub: Sixty seconds each: open, react, add something about you, and bridge when it is time.

heading: She Answers in Three
Words

lines.html: Hi Sarah, good to see you. How is your week going so far?
lines.speak: Hi Sarah, good to see you. How is your week going so far?

lines.html: I can imagine -- it is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it? Mine started badly: my flight was three hours late.
lines.speak: I can imagine. It is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it? Mine started badly: my flight was three hours late.

lines.html: Three hours? That is painful. Where were you flying from?
lines.speak: Three hours? That is painful. Where were you flying from?

lines.html: Sao Paulo. Four hours at the gate, and I read a whole book. By the way, did you have a good weekend?
lines.speak: Sao Paulo. Four hours at the gate, and I read a whole book. By the way, did you have a good weekend?

lines.html: I did, actually. I played tennis on Saturday. First time in two years.
lines.speak: I did, actually. I played tennis on Saturday. First time in two years.

lines.html: Oh really? I did not know you played. My daughter started last year, and now she beats me -- which is not good for my ego.
lines.speak: Oh really? I did not know you played. My daughter started last year, and now she beats me, which is not good for my ego.

lines.html: That happens fast. Wait until she is fifteen.
lines.speak: That happens fast. Wait until she is fifteen.

lines.html: I am not ready for that. Anyway, we have a full agenda today -- shall we start?
lines.speak: I am not ready for that. Anyway, we have a full agenda today. Shall we start?

comp: How does Sarah answer the first question?
In three words: "Busy. Same as always."
Diogo adds something about himself -- the three-hour flight delay.
How does Diogo end the small talk?
He says "anyway", mentions the full agenda, and asks if they can start.

quickfire.situation: Open the call. She is already there, camera on, silent.
tips: 'How is your week going so far?'
Never open with 'sorry I am late' if you are not.

quickfire.situation: She answers: 'Busy. Same as always.' Keep it alive.
tips: React, then add something about YOU.
A question tag helps: 'End of quarter for you too, isn't it?'

quickfire.situation: Ask about the weekend -- and add one detail about yours.
tips: Then answer your own question, in one line.

quickfire.situation: She says something you know nothing about (ice hockey).
tips: 'Oh really? I know nothing about it -- how does it work?'

quickfire.situation: She asks: 'So how are things in Brazil?' Answer in two sentences.
tips: One line about your city, one about the weather or food.

quickfire.situation: It is 3:02. Close the small talk and start the meeting.
tips: 'Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?'
Close it yourself. Do not wait for her.

items: So, how are things in Brazil these days?
Do you follow American football? No? What do you do for fun?
We have five minutes before the others join. Tell me something about yourself.
pressure.followup: Answer, react, and always add something about yourself. Never leave the other person doing all the work.

heading: The Two
Minutes
guided.prompt: It is 2:58. You and Sarah, alone on the call.
guided.sub: Use the warm-up card. Four moves, in this order:
chips: did you have a good weekend?

heading: She Gives You
Nothing
semi.sub: She answers every question in three words, twice. Do not give up and do not go silent: react, add something about yourself, and ask a different question. Then close the small talk yourself, at two minutes.

heading: Your Real
Client
free.headline: Two minutes. No notes. No agenda.
free.sub: Take a real person you talk to at Oracle -- an American colleague, a client, a vendor. Do the two minutes out loud: open, react, add something about you, ask one more question, and bridge to the agenda.

survival.en: How is your week going so far?
survival.pt: Como está sendo sua semana até agora?

survival.en: Busy, I can imagine. Mine started badly.
survival.pt: Corrida, imagino. A minha começou mal.

survival.en: Did you have a good weekend, by the way?
survival.pt: A propósito, teve um bom fim de semana?

survival.en: Oh really? I did not know you played tennis.
survival.pt: Sério? Eu não sabia que você jogava tênis.

survival.en: Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?
survival.pt: Enfim, temos uma pauta cheia. Vamos começar?

checks: I can open a call with a real question, not with silence.
I react, and then I add something about myself.
I can use question tags: "isn't it?", "doesn't it?"
I know the safe topics -- and the dangerous ones.
I close the small talk myself: "Anyway, shall we start?"

closing.line: Two minutes of nothing -- and now the client knows there is a person on the other side.
closing.next_title: Day 18 -- This Meeting Is Going Nowhere
closing.next_desc: Ending a meeting that stalls: park it, decide, next steps

order_audio.text: Diogo opens the call and asks how Sarah's week is going. She answers in three words, and he adds something about himself: his flight was three hours late. Then he asks about her weekend, and she says she played tennis. He reacts, and tells her that his daughter now beats him. Finally, he closes the small talk and starts the meeting.
items: She answers in three words, and he adds something about himself.
He reacts, and says his daughter now beats him at tennis.
Diogo opens the call and asks how her week is going.
He closes the small talk and starts the meeting.
He asks about her weekend, and she says she played tennis.

preclass.desc: Fill the two minutes before the call: open, react, add something about you, and bridge to the agenda.
preclass.context: It is 2:58, and the silence on the call is awkward. Somebody has to break the ice, and today it is Diogo. He keeps it casual: "How is your week going so far?" Sarah answers in three words -- busy, same as always. A weaker speaker would go silent here. Diogo does two things instead: he reacts and uses a question tag ("End of quarter for you too, isn't it?"), and then he adds something about himself: his flight was three hours late. Now it is a chat, not an interview. "By the way, did you have a good weekend?" She played tennis, and they catch up for a minute about his daughter. At two minutes, he closes it himself: "Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?"
quiz_ctx: Why does Diogo talk about his own flight?
Because if he only asks questions, it becomes an interview.
Because he wants to complain about the airline.
Because he is late for the meeting.
What does "Anyway" do at the end?
It closes the small talk and starts the meeting.
It changes the subject to another personal topic.
It asks the other person to speak more.
preclass.tip_sub: Os dois minutos antes da call: abrir, reagir, acrescentar algo seu e voltar à pauta. / The two minutes before the call.
tip_rows: How is your week going? (sujeito antes do verbo)
How is your week going so far?
Busy quarter for you too, isn't it?
preclass.tip_key: O "né?" brasileiro não existe em inglês: usa-se question tag (isn't it? / doesn't it? / didn't you?). E a regra de ouro do small talk: responda, e acrescente algo sobre você -- senão vira interrogatório.

blanks.phrase: How is your week going so far?

blanks.pre: "It is the end of the quarter for you too,
blanks.hint: Hint: question tag = o "né?" do inglês
blanks.phrase: It is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it?

blanks.hint: Hint: used to add or change topic / a propósito
blanks.phrase: By the way, did you have a good weekend?
blanks.post: , did you have a good weekend?"

blanks.pre: "Oh really? I did not know you
blanks.hint: Hint: past simple of play (reported after "did not know")
blanks.phrase: Oh really? I did not know you played tennis.

blanks.hint: Hint: closes the small talk / enfim
blanks.phrase: Anyway, we have a full agenda. Shall we start?
blanks.post: , we have a full agenda. Shall we start?"

blanks.phrase: Silence on a video call is awkward.

preclass.order_title: Put the Small Talk in Order
preclass.speech_intro: The five lines of a warm-up. Record each one. Light, warm, unhurried -- this is the only exercise where sounding relaxed IS the skill.
quiz_sit: You are alone on the call with the client, two minutes early. You:
Ask an open question: "How is your week going so far?"
Stay silent and wait for the others.
She answers: "Busy. Same as always." You:
React, and add something about your own week.
Ask another question immediately, then another.
Say nothing, and open your slides.
It is time to start. You say:
"Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?"
Nothing, and hope somebody else starts.
"Sorry for wasting your time with small talk."
think: Take a real American colleague or client. Do the two minutes out loud: (1) open with a real question, (2) react to the answer, (3) add something about your week, (4) ask one more question -- weekend, travel, sport, (5) bridge to the agenda. Record it.
Now she gives you nothing: "Fine. Busy. Same as always." Keep the conversation alive out loud for one more minute, without going silent and without asking three questions in a row. Record it.

complementary.title: Up in the Air (2009)
complementary.desc: Ryan Bingham starts conversations with strangers all day. Watch how easy questions open a friendly talk.
complementary.tip: Note 3 opening questions from the film and use them before your next call.

complementary.title: BEP 44c -- Socializing: Starting a Conversation
complementary.desc: How to break the ice, introduce yourself, and keep an easy conversation on a safe topic.
complementary.tip: Practise the dialog out loud, then prepare 3 safe topics for your next American call.

complementary.title: How to Make Small Talk at a Business Meeting
complementary.desc: Ready-to-use small talk sentences for the minutes before a business meeting starts.
complementary.tip: Learn 5 phrases by heart and use two of them at the start of your next meeting.

teacher.title: Abertura (2 min): Compartilhe a tela. Diga: 'Last class you drew a line with a client. Today there is no agenda, nothing to defend -- and for most engineers this is the hardest English there is.' Avise: hoje ele fala quase o tempo todo.
teacher.warmup: Warm-up falado (4 min): Toque a frase da Aula 16. Depois a virada: 'Two minutes, no agenda, camera on. Go.' Faca a pergunta do box e cale a boca 90 segundos.
teacher.goal: Objetivo (1 min): 'Words, one move, and then two minutes of silence with me -- three times.' Rapido.
teacher.vocab_transition: Transicao vocab (1 min): 'Eight words. Click each card.' Ferramenta, nao aula de vocabulario.
teacher.vocab1: Vocab reveal 1-4 (4 min): Pista, tentativa, reveal, audio, uma frase real. CCQ 'break the ice': 'Is it the first conversation or the last? (The first.)' CCQ 'catch up': 'Do you catch up with a stranger? (No -- with someone you know.)' CCQ 'awkward': 'Is awkward dangerous or uncomfortable? (Uncomfortable.)'
teacher.vocab2: Vocab reveal 5-8 (4 min): Mesma dinamica. CCQ 'by the way': 'Does it add a topic or close it? (Adds.)' CCQ 'anyway': 'Does it open or close the small talk? (Closes.)' Reforce a diferenca entre os dois -- e o erro classico.
teacher.vocab_check: Vocab check falado (4 min): 90 segundos sobre uma call em que ninguem falou nada e ele nao soube o que dizer. Minimo cinco palavras novas. Voce so escuta.
teacher.ch3: Transicao (1 min): 'The camera is on. She is there. Nobody is speaking.' Deixe o silencio doer -- 5 segundos reais de silencio.
teacher.listening1: Listening 1 (4 min): 'Sophia explains why the silence costs you the client.' Toque uma vez, sem texto. Perguntas no proximo slide.
teacher.listening1_comp: Comprehension falada (3 min): Respostas em voz alta antes do clique. Depois: 'Which topics would be dangerous with an American client?' Deixe ele listar falando.
teacher.artefact: Artefato (3 min): Nao leia por ele. 'Read the warm-up card, then do the two minutes with me, now.' Cronometre 2 minutos de verdade. Voce responde curto de proposito.
teacher.grammar: Grammar discovery (4 min): Toque as 4 frases. Pergunta unica: 'Which one opens? Which one keeps it alive? Which one ends it?' Guie: How is your week going = abre; isn't it = mantem; anyway = fecha.
teacher.rule: Reveal the Rule (3 min): Clique. Abrir, reagir, voltar. Treine 4 question tags em voz alta (isn't it / doesn't it / didn't you / aren't you). CCQ: 'How is going your week -- correct? (No.)'
teacher.mistake: Common Mistake (2 min): ordem da pergunta e o "ne?". Peca as versoes certas em voz alta, duas vezes.
teacher.bank: Useful language falado (4 min): Cada frase lida em voz alta e imediatamente adaptada a vida REAL dele (voo, filha, fim de semana). Seis frases da tela, seis frases dele.
teacher.ch5: Transicao (1 min): 'She is distracted, and she answers in three words. Most people give up here.' Suba a tensao.
teacher.dialogue: Dialogo (6 min): Voce e a Sarah e voce responde CURTO ('Busy. Same as always.'). Antes de cada fala do Diogo, ELE fala primeiro; so entao clique e toque o audio. Repare no momento em que ela abre: quando ele fala de si.
teacher.dialogue_comp: Dialogue comprehension (2 min): Perguntas sobre a SARAH. Depois: 'What exactly made her open up?' Deixe ele responder falando -- e a licao da aula.
teacher.listening2: Listening 2 (4 min): 'Two minutes, done well.' Toque uma vez. Depois: 'How many times did he talk about himself? And how did he close it?' -- ele fala.
teacher.scenarios: Scenarios falados (5 min): Tres situacoes, 60 segundos cada, sem preparo. Voce responde curto e frio. Nao corrija no meio.
teacher.ch6: Transicao (1 min): 'From here you break the ice, and I give you nothing. Three times.'
teacher.quickfire: Quick Fire (5 min): Uma situacao por vez, resposta imediata. Sem correcao entre elas -- fluencia primeiro.
teacher.rp_guided: Role-play GUIADO (4 min): Voce e a Sarah. Ele conduz os dois minutos com os quatro movimentos. Cronometre. Se ele nao falar NADA sobre si, mande refazer.
teacher.rp_semi: Role-play SEMI-LIVRE (5 min): Voce da respostas de tres palavras, duas vezes. Ele nao pode ir ao silencio nem disparar perguntas: reage, fala de si, muda de assunto e fecha aos 2 minutos.
teacher.rp_free: Role-play LIVRE (5 min): Sem apoio. 'A real client. Two minutes. Then bridge to the agenda. Go.' NAO interrompa, cronometre e diga o tempo no fim.
teacher.pressure: Under pressure (4 min): Tres perguntas pessoais, uma a uma. Se ele responder e parar (sem acrescentar nada), repita: 'And you? Give me more.'
teacher.survival: Survival Card (3 min): 'Five phrases. With these, no silence lasts more than three seconds.' Toque cada uma; tom leve e relaxado -- e o unico exercicio em que soar relaxado E a habilidade.
teacher.learned: Wrap-up (3 min): Ele clica cada check e diz a frase que prova. So depois devolva as 2 correcoes. Homework ORAL: gravar 2 minutos de small talk com um cliente real (abrir, reagir, falar de si, fechar).
teacher.closing: Fechamento (1 min): Homework ORAL: gravar 2 minutos de warm-up e enviar. Anuncie a Aula 18: 'Next time: the meeting that never ends -- and how you stop it.'
